channel.title: AV Pain Reliever
channel.description: AV Pain Reliever — release feed for the Sparkle auto-updater.

item.title: Version 0.1.11
item.description:
What's new

  • The update window now comes to the front when you click Check for Updates instead of hiding behind your other apps.
  • The update window has a proper "Software Update" title and a What's New panel sourced from each release.

item.title: Version 0.1.12
item.description:
What's new

Listen, the old icon was two pills, playing it cute. The new icon is one refined capsule on a charcoal squircle — same brand wink, way more "I'm a serious utility." Big improvement, just look at it.

Also — and this is the real one — there's a Menu bar icon picker now. Settings → General. Eighteen SF symbols to choose from. Mic, camera, sliders, waveforms, switching arrows, the works. We even snuck a party popper in there, because why not — the About window already throws confetti, we're keeping the bit going.

Quick technical thing: the picker turns off when the per-profile icon setting is on. The active profile's icon is already doing the work; don't want two settings tripping over each other — that's amateur hour. The caption tells you exactly what's happening.

That's the update. New icon, new picker, eighteen options. Money.

item.title: Version 0.1.13
item.description:
What's new

Listen, the switch notification — the little banner that pops up when you change docks — used to show our app icon. Just the app icon. Every time. Fine, but generic. We're upgrading. Now the notification shows the active profile's icon: house for home office, laptop for laptop, whatever you set. Per-profile. Per-switch. Sharp.

The "new location detected" notification gets the question-mark glyph too, same one the menu bar shows. Consistent system, top to bottom.

That's the deal. New thumbnail on the toast. Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0
item.description:
What's new

OK here's the deal. v0.2.0 is the big one. AV Pain Reliever is now a virtual camera. That's right — the same app that's been quietly swapping your audio defaults all this time can now also be the camera that Zoom, Slack, Teams, and any other video app picks up. One camera in their picker. One name. AV Pain Reliever. It streams whatever real camera the active profile names — your built-in webcam at the laptop, the HDMI capture at the home office, your iPhone via Continuity at the conference room — whatever you've configured. The profile changes, the picture follows. Money.

Here's what this fixes: Zoom, Slack, and Teams keep their own camera selection that ignores the system default. So back in v0.1.x when you docked at a new location, your microphone and speakers would change, but Zoom would just sit there showing your laptop webcam like nothing happened. That's amateur hour. With v0.2.0, you pick "AV Pain Reliever" in Zoom once and you're done. Forever.

It's off by default — installing a virtual camera is a real thing and we're not turning it on without your permission, that's not how we do business. Open Settings → Camera and flip the toggle. macOS will ask you to approve the extension once (System Settings → Login Items & Extensions → Camera Extensions). After that, you're in.

A few technical things, briefly, because if you're reading this far you probably care:

  • Hold-last-frame during the swap. When you change profiles, the new camera takes a beat to warm up — about half a second. Instead of flashing black at your Zoom call, the virtual camera holds the last frame from the old camera until the new one's delivering frames. You see a soft pause; nobody on the call sees a glitch.
  • Format conversion for any camera. Built-in webcams ship BGRA, USB capture cards ship YUV at 1080p, every device has its quirks. We accept whatever the camera natively delivers and convert to 1280×720 BGRA on the fly, hardware-accelerated where possible. Means your weird HDMI capture card just works.
  • Profile-driven everywhere. The same Add Profile wizard that picks your audio defaults now also picks the camera the virtual camera streams. No new config to learn — it's the same camera field you've already been using. v0.1.x configs upgrade clean.

One known thing: if you toggle the virtual camera off and then back on inside the same launch, macOS holds the extension in a stale state and the feed goes black. The Settings UI catches this and gives you a Restart AV Pain Reliever button that handles it. One click, fresh process, you're back. Edge case — most folks turn it on once and leave it. Not a deal-breaker.

That's the update. Virtual camera, profile-driven, hold-last-frame, universal format support. v0.1.x will keep getting patch releases in parallel for anyone who doesn't need any of this. Money.

item.title: Version 0.1.14
item.description:
What's new

Listen, here's the deal. v0.2.0 shipped yesterday with the virtual camera — Zoom, Slack, Teams, the whole crew finally following your profile. Big release. But here's the thing: a virtual camera is a system extension, which means macOS asks for approval, the in-Settings toggle has a known quirk, and frankly we're not ready to push that on every user as the default upgrade. That's not how we do business.

So v0.1.14 brings you a switch. Settings → General → Updates → "Receive experimental updates." Off by default — you keep getting the steady stream of v0.1.x patches like nothing happened. Flip it on, and you're now on the early-access track: v0.2.0's virtual camera shows up as your next upgrade, you go through the system-extension dance, you're streaming whatever camera the active profile names into Zoom. Money. Flip it back off, you're back on the stable line. No commitments. Pick your speed.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.1
item.description:
What's new

Quick patch. v0.1.14 just shipped the "Receive experimental updates" toggle that lets v0.1.x users opt into v0.2.x — but if you're already on v0.2.0, you didn't have that toggle yet. Catch-22. v0.2.0.1 fixes it: Settings → General → Updates now has the same toggle you saw in v0.1.14, defaulting off. Flip it on if you want future v0.2.x patches to roll in automatically; leave it off and you stay on v0.2.0.1 until something stable enough hits the main line. Pick your speed. Money.

item.title: Version 0.1.15
item.description:
What's new

Quick fix. Some USB capture cards — looking at you, the cheap HDMI-to-USB ones — expose their serial number through one IOKit key but not the other. AV Pain Reliever was only checking one of those keys, so if your profile fingerprint pinned a specific serial for a card like that, we'd silently miss the match and fall back to your laptop profile every time. Worked great for everything except the cards that mattered. Patched. Now we check both keys, the serial comes through, your fingerprint matches when you dock, the right profile applies. Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.2
item.description:
What's new

Same fix as v0.1.15 — that USB serial-number gotcha where some capture cards only expose one of the two IOKit keys (the raw kUSBSerialNumberString instead of the friendly "USB Serial Number"). Now we read both. If your dock has one of those quirky cards in the fingerprint and the engine was mysteriously falling back to your laptop profile every relaunch, this is the fix. Dock, launch, the right profile applies. Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.3
item.description:
What's new

UX cleanup. Before this, if you were running the experimental v0.2.x with the "Receive experimental updates" toggle off, clicking Check for Updates would tell you "you're up to date" and report v0.1.15 as the newest available — even though you're running v0.2.0, which is higher. Confusing. Technically Sparkle was filtering the appcast to stable-only because no channels were allowlisted, but nobody reads it that way.

Now the dialog explains the actual situation. Click Check for Updates on an experimental build with the toggle off, you get a tailored alert with three buttons: Enable Experimental Updates (flips the switch and runs the check fresh, this time seeing experimental items), Check Stable Anyway (the old behavior, for the rare case you want to confirm what stable says), or Cancel. One click to actually be on the experimental track instead of squinting at a contradictory dialog. Money.

item.title: Version 0.1.16
item.description:
What's new

Wizard refinements. Two of them, one release.

The device list in Add Profile is sorted properly now. Location-defining gear — mics, speakers, cameras, capture cards, your dock, displays — floats to the top. Travel peripherals — keyboards, mice, phones, AirPods — sink to the bottom with their existing Suggested: untick pill. No more squinting past Apple Magic Keyboard to find Yeti Stereo Microphone.

Also: if you untick every device in the list, the wizard now tells you what that means. Zero devices = fallback profile = matches whenever no other profile does (typically your laptop, undocked). Before, it just showed the generic "uncheck the peripherals" caption regardless of state. Now it flips to a labeled hint the second you clear the selection — so if you didn't mean to ship a fallback, the wizard catches you. Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.4
item.description:
What's new

Brings the wizard refinements from v0.1.16 over to the experimental track. Two improvements you'll feel the next time you click Add Profile:

The device list is sorted properly now. Mics, speakers, cameras, capture cards, your dock, displays — anything that defines a location — float to the top. Travel peripherals (keyboards, mice, phones, AirPods) sink to the bottom with their existing Suggested: untick pill. The hardware that defines your dock is right where you'd look first.

And if you untick everything in the list, the wizard now tells you what that means: zero devices = fallback profile, matches whenever no other profile does (typically your laptop, undocked). The caption flips to a labeled hint the second you clear the selection — so if you didn't mean to ship a fallback, you'll catch it before saving. Money.

item.title: Version 0.1.17
item.description:
What's new

Two more wizard polish items, both shaken loose by real-world testing on the docked-mac.

The device list sort actually does what the v0.1.16 release notes said it does now. Turns out the previous tiering was only catching the bottom — keyboards, mice, phones — leaving everything else jumbled in alphabetical order. Mics and audio interfaces would just sit wherever the alphabet put them. Fixed: there's now a positive priority tier at the top for anything with "microphone", "audio", "speaker", "camera", "capture", or "display" in its name. Three real tiers — priority, neutral, portable — with alphabetical within each. So Blue Microphones and CalDigit Thunderbolt 3 Audio finally land where they belong: top of the list, where you can see them.

Also: the wizard has been quietly auto-filling profile names from your dock signature for a while now (CalDigit detected → "Home Office", Yeti detected → "Studio", and so on). Behaviorally helpful, visually mysterious — looks like the wizard read your mind. Now the slug-preview caption prefixes with a bolded "Suggested." when the name came from auto-fill. Edit the name to anything and the prefix drops. So you know what the wizard did, and you know it's a guess you can override. Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.5
item.description:
What's new

Brings the v0.1.17 wizard polish over to the experimental track. Two improvements you'll see the next time you click Add Profile:

The device list sort actually does what the v0.2.0.4 release notes said it does. Previously the tier logic was only catching keyboards and mice at the bottom — leaving mics and audio interfaces in alphabetical position with everything else. Now there's a real priority tier at the top: anything with "microphone", "audio", "speaker", "camera", "capture", or "display" in the name floats up. Three tiers (priority → neutral → portable), alphabetical within each. Blue Microphones and CalDigit Thunderbolt 3 Audio land where they belong.

Also: the wizard's quiet auto-fill of profile names from your dock signature is now visible. CalDigit attached → "Home Office" used to just appear in the field. Now the slug-preview caption prefixes with a bolded "Suggested." when the value came from auto-fill, and the prefix drops the moment you edit the name. Less mysterious, same labor savings. Money.

item.title: Version 0.1.18
item.description:
What's new

The Add Profile wizard got smart enough to do most of the work for you. Three coordinated upgrades, all in one release.

The wizard used to start with every USB device on your dock pre-ticked — your microphone, your dock, your monitor, your keyboard, your trackpad, your phone, all checked. Your job was to scan the list and untick the stuff that travels with you. Easy to forget, easy to wind up with a Magic Keyboard quietly hardcoded into your home-office fingerprint. Now it flips: AV Pain Reliever pre-ticks only the headline hardware (microphones, cameras, capture cards, dedicated audio interfaces — the stuff that genuinely defines a location) and leaves everything else unticked. You actively tick what else matters. Cleaner fingerprints, less squinting.

To make the new defaults legible at a glance, every row in the device list now wears one of three states. Green "Important: " pill on the auto-ticked headline gear (Important: microphone, Important: capture card, Important: audio interface). Muted gray "Travels with you" pill on portable peripherals — keyboards, mice, phones, AirPods, watches — auto-unticked, the pill explains why your iPhone is in the list but not selected. No pill on neutral things like hub legs, displays, card readers, Stream Decks — quietly available, tick if you want them in your fingerprint. The old yellow "Suggested: untick" nudge is gone — once the suggestion is the default, the nudge is redundant.

Also: the wizard's profile-name auto-suggest finally checks if you already have a profile by that name. CalDigit dock attached used to silently fill in "Home Office" even if you already had a Home Office profile, queueing up a save-time collision dialog you had to fight through. Now it suppresses the suggestion and leaves the field empty — because if you're hitting Add Profile, you want a new profile, and pre-loading a name you already use is just signing you up for an argument with the wizard. Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.6
item.description:
What's new

Brings the v0.1.18 wizard rework over to the experimental track.

The Add Profile wizard used to start with every USB device pre-ticked and ask you to scan for the ones to remove. Now it flips: only the headline hardware (microphones, cameras, capture cards, dedicated audio interfaces) is auto-ticked. Everything else is visible but unticked — you actively tick anything else that defines this location.

To make it legible, every device row wears one of three states. Green "Important: " on the auto-ticked headline gear. Muted gray "Travels with you" on portable peripherals (keyboards, mice, phones) — auto-unticked, the pill explains why your iPhone is in the list but not selected. No pill on neutral things like hub legs, displays, card readers — quietly available. The old yellow "Suggested: untick" nudge retired; once unticked is the default, the nudge is just noise.

Plus: the wizard's profile-name auto-suggest finally checks for collisions. CalDigit attached used to silently fill in "Home Office" even when you already had one. Now it suppresses the suggestion if the slug is already in your config — leaving the field empty so you actually name your new profile. Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.7
item.description:
What's new

Two big quality-of-life wins on the virtual camera.

The green camera light used to stay on the whole time the app was open. Just… lit. Like someone was watching. Not anymore — capture only fires up when something's actually reading the virtual camera. Zoom call ends? Light's off in 30 seconds. Idle for an hour? Light's been off for fifty-nine and a half. The way it should be.

The other thing: the profile camera picker no longer lets you pick "AV Pain Reliever" from the list. Why would you? It's the OUTPUT. The thing Zoom is pointed at. Your profile names the REAL camera — the BRIO, the FaceTime HD, the capture card — and AV Pain Reliever routes it through. Plus, when the virtual camera is on, macOS's system camera preference now also points at AV Pain Reliever, so FaceTime and Safari follow the same routing as Zoom. One source of truth. Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.8
item.description:
What's new

Conference room save. The Neat Bar at the office was making the virtual camera freeze on one frame — sometimes, when you opened Zoom AFTER docking. Behind the scenes, the host was opening AV Pain Reliever as its OWN source. Self-source. Feedback loop. The frame you saw was just the same cached image echoing through the loop forever. Not great.

Two fixes. The host now refuses to ever open the virtual camera as a source — that's its own output, would be like recording yourself recording yourself. And when a profile says "use the Neat Bar" but Zoom isn't open yet, we hold onto that — so when Zoom DOES connect, the host opens the Neat Bar on the first try. Not the virtual camera. Not whatever the system thinks is preferred. The right camera. First try. Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.9
item.description:
What's new

Three things in one drop.

A new Stats tab in Settings. Opt-in, off by default — you flip the switch when you want it on. After that, every profile switch, manual override, streak day, and unique USB device gets counted locally. No content, no telemetry, no phoning home. Just numbers about your setup, on your Mac. The "Most-used location" line is the one that hits.

The Add and Edit Profile windows finally match Settings visually. They were lined up wrong — gray cards in the wizard pushed against the window edges while Settings had breathing room. Took a few iterations to nail. Now they're family.

Cleaner pills in the wizard's USB list. "mic" instead of "microphone." "video" covers cameras AND HDMI capture cards (capture cards aren't really cameras but they're video sources from your point of view — one word covers it). "audio" for the catch-all. And the list itself sorts smart: important stuff at the top, the "(unnamed device)" entries (those internal hub-leg things you never tick) pushed to the bottom. Easier to scan in two seconds. Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.10
item.description:
What's new

Polish drop. No new features — pure surface work. The kind of release where you open Settings, look around, and your shoulders drop a quarter-inch.

The wizard footer. Cancel used to be a button-with-an-X, Save was a button-with-a-plus. Apple's Cancel buttons have never had icons, never will. Ours shouldn't either. Fixed. Both are clean text now, the way every native sheet on macOS is.

The Profiles tab. The "Add Profile" button at the bottom isn't a big blue chunk anymore — it's a quiet bordered "Add Profile…" with an ellipsis, the way System Settings actually does it. The pencil and trash on each profile row lost their bordered chips. Just icons, no chrome, rows can breathe. Looks like Mail. Looks like Reminders.

The "Restart required" state on the Camera tab used to put a tiny grey button next to a big orange dot — the dot yelling, the button whispering. Now they match. And the Reset stats button on the Stats tab is finally a full-width red row inside its grouped section, the way Apple ID does Sign Out. Red text, full row, no ambiguity about which button is the dangerous one.

Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.11
item.description:
What's new

Polish drop. One fix, but it's been quietly bugging us for months.

The bottom of the Profiles tab. That strip with the Add Profile button and the "4 profiles" count? It's been a faux bottom bar all this time — a custom HStack with a Divider above it, dressed up to LOOK like a native Mac bar but never actually being one. The button inside always read a little off, a little flat, a little not-quite-Apple. Could never quite put a finger on why.

Fixed. It's a real macOS bottom bar now — translucent material chrome, automatic separator, the same kind of bar Mail uses under the mailbox sidebar and Reminders uses under the lists. And the Add Profile affordance is now the universal Apple + icon at bottom-left, the way Mail does new mailboxes, Reminders does new lists, and System Settings → Network does interface +/-. Hover it for the tooltip. Borderless, minimal, instantly recognizable.

Money.

item.title: Version 0.2.0.12
item.description:
What's new

Rendering fix. Buttons throughout the app got slightly cleaner — flatter, more native. Same intent, more polish.

What actually happened: every release until this one got compiled on GitHub's CI with an older Xcode that drew SwiftUI buttons one way; the dev's Mac has a newer Xcode that drew them the other way. The dev kept seeing the new look on his screen and accidentally shipping the old look to everyone else. Same source code, two different runtime paths into SwiftUI. Apple keeps both alive forever for backward compatibility, so the SDK at compile time picks which one your binary calls.

Now the build pipeline runs on the dev's actual Mac, with the actual Xcode he develops with. What lands in your /Applications is now the same look he sees on his own screen. Same toolchain, same SwiftUI runtime path, every time.

Money.
